video_title: Wall Street Week | USMCA: Can North America’s Trade Deal Survive?
gist: A Bloomberg Wall Street Week special on the USMCA review (sunset/6-year review around July 1) walks autos, agriculture and energy. Consensus across CEOs, economists and former negotiators: the deal stays in force even without renewal, the real adversary is China (transshipment, EV dumping), and the prize is certainty/duration (a 16-year renewal) over headline terms. Expect aggressive new auto rules-of-origin with domestic-content and near-zero China content; energy is best left untouched.

themes.theme: Auto rules-of-origin and Fortress North America

insights.claim: The Trump administration is expected to push aggressive USMCA rules-of-origin changes adding a US domestic-content requirement and near-zero Chinese-component content for a car to qualify for tariff-free treatment.
insights.speaker: Kelly Ann Shaw, former USTR General Counsel
insights.so_what: Tighter rules-of-origin and domestic-content thresholds reset cost curves for OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers and gate which vehicles keep duty-free access; watch parts suppliers with deep China content.
insights.quote: the administration wants a very aggressive change to the rules of origin, which would actually also have a domestic content requirement... And almost none of that car should be made by any components from China.

insights.claim: North American auto supply chains are so deeply integrated that a single Linamar part crosses the US-Mexico-Canada borders multiple times for sequential processing before final distribution, making decoupling impractical.
insights.speaker: Jim Jarrell, CEO of Linamar
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that tariff disruption hits the same part repeatedly; investors should model cumulative, not single, border friction on integrated suppliers.
insights.quote: the original part that we get is a forging that comes into Mexico, which goes into the U.S. ... comes into Canada ... back to the U.S. ... over to Canada where we do the sort of final assembly... You can't unbake the omelet

insights.claim: Without NAFTA/USMCA economies of scale there might be no North American car industry at all, and a US-only car would be more expensive, less innovative, and unable to compete with imports from Japan, South Korea and Europe.
insights.speaker: Shannon O'Neil, CFR director of studies
insights.so_what: Frames reshoring as cost-inflationary and competitively weakening; bearish signal for US auto competitiveness if integration unwinds.
insights.quote: There's a real question if we didn't have NAFTA, if we didn't have USMCA, would we have a North American car industry at all

insights.claim: Trump frames the auto industry negotiation as a zero-sum game where only one party can keep the industry, signaling a confrontational posture in the review.
insights.speaker: Kelly Ann Shaw, former USTR General Counsel
insights.so_what: A zero-sum framing raises odds of contentious terms and tariff brinkmanship; expect volatility in cross-border auto names during the talks.
insights.quote: President Trump was describing it as a zero-sum game. He wants the auto industry. I want the auto industry. Only one of us is going to have it.

insights.claim: Roughly 40 cents of every dollar Mexico exports to the United States accrues to a US company, complicating the headline trade-deficit narrative.
insights.speaker: Geronimo Gutierrez Fernandez, former Mexican Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: US firms capture much of Mexican export value, meaning punitive Mexico tariffs partly tax US companies; relevant for US multinationals with Mexican operations.
insights.quote: around 40% on every dollar that is exported from Mexico into the United States it's actually a U.S. company.

themes.theme: China as the real adversary and transshipment

insights.claim: All USMCA parties are open to building a 'Fortress North America' against China, with transshipment of Chinese parts through Mexico into the US a central negotiating concern.
insights.speaker: Shannon O'Neil, CFR director of studies
insights.so_what: A coordinated anti-China bloc reshapes sourcing rules and could redirect supply chains away from Chinese inputs; watch for stricter origin verification.
insights.quote: all parties are open to, creating a real North America fortress vis-a-vis China

insights.claim: Chinese imports into Mexico have grown dramatically over the past five years, prompting Mexico to push back against Chinese cars, car parts and electronics to protect North American integration.
insights.speaker: Shannon O'Neil, CFR director of studies
insights.so_what: Mexico aligning against China lowers backdoor-access risk and supports a tighter bloc; signals reduced Chinese export channel via Mexico.
insights.quote: China... have grown dramatically over the last five years into Mexico. Some of this are cars and car parts and the like

insights.claim: Nobel economist Paul Krugman, a longtime free-trade advocate, now believes conditional tariffs on Chinese cars are probably necessary to prevent Europe's auto industry from being hollowed out.
insights.speaker: Paul Krugman, economist
insights.so_what: A pro-globalization economist endorsing targeted Chinese auto tariffs signals a durable consensus shift toward intervention; bullish for protected EU/NA automakers, bearish for Chinese EV exporters.
insights.quote: I do think that conditional tariffs on Chinese cars are probably going to be necessary. I don't think that the Europeans can allow their auto industry to be totally hollowed out.

insights.claim: Krugman argues interdependence can now be weaponized against the US, justifying maintaining strategic capacity domestically or in reliable allies.
insights.speaker: Paul Krugman, economist
insights.so_what: Security-driven onshoring of strategic goods is becoming bipartisan/cross-ideological; tailwind for domestic capacity and allied-shoring plays.
insights.quote: interdependence can be weaponized. We used to think that that was something we did to other countries, but now we find out that other countries do it to us, too.

insights.claim: Industry participants would prefer higher but committed/stable tariffs over rolling annual renegotiations, valuing predictability above tariff levels.
insights.speaker: Paul Krugman, economist
insights.so_what: Markets and capex decisions hinge on policy stability; a 16-year renewal would unlock delayed investment more than marginal tariff cuts.
insights.quote: I would almost prefer that Trump put on more tariffs on Canada and Mexico, but committed to keep them in place, than have rolling negotiations where every year you don't know what next year will be like.

insights.claim: Even if parties do not agree to renew the USMCA by July 1, the existing agreement stays in force while modifications are negotiated, making a no-deal collapse unlikely.
insights.speaker: Kirsten Hillman, former Canadian Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: A legal backstop reduces tail risk of an abrupt trade-rules vacuum; near-term disruption fears around the deadline are likely overstated.
insights.quote: even if on July 1st the parties don't agree to renew it, it stays in place... So I think the chances of the deal staying in place are very high.

insights.claim: A renewed USMCA would extend roughly 16 years with the next review in 6 years, the mechanism the sunset clause was designed to trigger.
insights.speaker: Kirsten Hillman, former Canadian Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: Defines the actual stability horizon businesses are pricing; a successful renewal buys ~16 years of long-term investment confidence.
insights.quote: It sort of extends it for 16 years with another review in 6 years.

insights.claim: Trade uncertainty is causing businesses to delay long-term capital investment decisions because they cannot price future tariff costs and market access.
insights.speaker: Tom Halverson, CEO of CoBank
insights.so_what: Policy ambiguity is directly suppressing capex; resolution would release pent-up investment in trade-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: people don't know they're delaying making investment decisions... because they don't know if that investment decision is going to be wise or not

insights.claim: Former ambassador argues the benefits of USMCA have not yet fully shown because it took effect in 2020 just as COVID hit, with a real pickup in trade and employment only emerging now.
insights.speaker: Geronimo Gutierrez Fernandez, former Mexican Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: Implies upside is still ahead if the deal persists; bullish for intra-regional trade volumes on continuation.
insights.quote: The agreement went into effect in 2020, right at the time that COVID hit... After that stabilized, you're beginning to see... a pickup in trade and employment

insights.claim: The nine major US crops have all been producing at a loss with commodity prices down about 30% over the last three years.
insights.speaker: Stu Swanson, Iowa corn/soy/pig farmer, Iowa Corn Growers board
insights.so_what: Severe farm-income distress raises odds of policy support and shapes ag-input, equipment and land demand; bearish near-term ag economics.
insights.quote: over the last three years, we've seen about a 30 percent decline in commodity prices... we've seen the nine major crops all be underwater.

insights.claim: Mexico and Canada are now the top two export markets for US agriculture, with US ag exports to them up about 600% since NAFTA's inception, while China has slipped to third since 2023.
insights.speaker: Tom Halverson / David Westin
insights.so_what: US ag is now structurally dependent on North American buyers, not China; retaliation by Canada/Mexico would be far more damaging to farmers than China cuts.
insights.quote: Since NAFTA to today... U.S. ag exports to Mexico and Canada have grown about 600 percent.

insights.claim: Mexico is the largest export market for US corn, taking about 6.5% of total US production, and combined US-Mexico corn/corn-product trade is about 1.8 billion bushels—equal to Minnesota's entire output.
insights.speaker: Stu Swanson, Iowa farmer
insights.so_what: Losing the Mexico corn channel would roughly double US carryout stocks; a quantified downside scenario for corn prices if USMCA fails.
insights.quote: The two countries combined, when you look at corn and corn products, it's about 1.8 billion bushels of corn, which is the entire production of the state of Minnesota

insights.claim: About 20% of total US agricultural product must be exported and, with no US population growth, the long-term future of US agriculture is defined as exports, exports and more exports.
insights.speaker: Tom Halverson, CEO of CoBank
insights.so_what: Domestic demand cannot absorb US ag output, so export-market access is existential; trade policy is the primary swing factor for farm profitability.
insights.quote: the long-term future for U.S. agriculture, it can be defined as exports, exports and more exports.

insights.claim: Despite USMCA success, the US runs an agricultural trade deficit with both Mexico and Canada, driven by year-round consumer demand for imported fruits and vegetables, creating a potential sticking point for the Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Tom Halverson, CEO of CoBank
insights.so_what: An ag deficit gives the administration a pretext to press ag terms; watch for produce-import friction or tariff threats.
insights.quote: we want them 365 days a year. That means we have to buy them and import them... our agricultural balance of trade going negative.

insights.claim: Even after a $12 billion Trump aid package, farm conditions worsened, with financing drying up for 2026 crops and farmer suicides reported in Iowa.
insights.speaker: Stu Swanson / David Westin
insights.so_what: Aid is not stabilizing farm finances; credit stress signals risk for ag lenders and continued consolidation/exits in farming.
insights.quote: even after a $12 billion aid package... We've seen farmers that weren't able to get financing for the 2026 crop.

themes.theme: Energy: two bilateral machines best left untouched

insights.claim: North American energy operates as two large bilateral relationships, with 60% of US oil imports coming from Canada (refineries tuned for Canadian sour crude) and 70% of Mexico's imported natural gas coming from the US.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquin-Bitar, CSIS
insights.so_what: Deep, asset-specific interdependence (sour-crude refineries, gas pipelines) makes energy disruption costly and slow to reroute; supports status-quo continuity.
insights.quote: 60% of U.S. oil imports come from Canada. The refineries in the U.S. are adapted in order to refine that sour crude oil from Canada.

insights.claim: 60% of Mexico's electricity is generated from natural gas, the majority of which is imported from the US, making Mexican power generation directly dependent on US gas.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquin-Bitar, CSIS
insights.so_what: US gas underpins Mexican manufacturing competitiveness; gas pipeline operators and LNG/feedgas exposure benefit from continued flows.
insights.quote: 70% of the natural gas that Mexico imports yearly comes from the U.S. 60% of the electricity generated in Mexico comes from natural gas.

insights.claim: USMCA dropped NAFTA's energy chapter and proportionality clause, and the open question is whether the US will seek to restore an energy clause given the current global energy situation and AI-driven power demand.
insights.speaker: Gitane De Silva, former CEO of the Canada Energy Regulator
insights.so_what: A reinstated energy/proportionality clause would lock in Canadian energy access for the US; relevant to Canadian producers and cross-border pipeline economics.
insights.quote: Will the U.S. be looking to return to some type of energy clause in a renewed USMCA?

insights.claim: Mexico's 2014 energy liberalization under Peña Nieto was reversed by López Obrador in 2018, and constitutional amendments under Sheinbaum now give state firms Pemex and CFE priority over US investors, a top USMCA friction point.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquin-Bitar, CSIS
insights.so_what: National-treatment violations by Pemex/CFE preference threaten US energy investors in Mexico; a key deal-breaker risk and signal of limited Mexican energy openness.
insights.quote: they are giving Pemex and CFE a predominant position in Mexico's energy market. And that's one of the biggest obstacles that we have for the USMCA review.

insights.claim: Energy-sector players want energy kept out of the USMCA renegotiation entirely because the current free flow is mutually growth-positive.
insights.speaker: Mike Howard, CEO of Howard Energy Partners
insights.so_what: Industry preference for status quo lowers odds of disruptive energy provisions; supportive for existing cross-border energy infrastructure cash flows.
insights.quote: We probably want to make sure that energy stays out of the conversation, because right now the free flow of energy that's going on right now is helping the economy grow.

insights.claim: More than 90% of Canada's energy exports go to the United States and over 70% of all Canadian exports go south, leaving Canada unable to replace the US market overnight despite diversification efforts.
insights.speaker: Gitane De Silva, former CEO of the Canada Energy Regulator
insights.so_what: Canada's structural dependence (pipelines built north-south) limits its negotiating leverage and makes diversification a slow, multiyear story.
insights.quote: more than 70% of our exports still go to the United States, more than 90% of our energy exports. We cannot replace that market overnight.

insights.claim: US trade deficits remain the president's 'North Star,' and they have not improved under USMCA—roughly flat with Canada (~$46B in 2025) and rising to nearly $200B with Mexico—making the deficit the central deal risk.
insights.speaker: Kelly Ann Shaw, former USTR General Counsel
insights.so_what: Because the administration anchors on deficits the deal could be reshaped regardless of efficiency gains; watch Mexico-targeted measures most closely.
insights.quote: With Mexico, it's increased to almost $200 billion. That is not sustainable from the president's perspective... it is his North Star.

insights.claim: Total North American intra-bloc trade has risen about 50% since USMCA entered into force in 2020.
insights.speaker: Kelly Ann Shaw, former USTR General Counsel
insights.so_what: Quantifies the integration the deal underpins; the scale of intra-bloc trade is what is at stake if rules unwind.
insights.quote: We've seen trade increase 50% within the North American trading bloc since 2020 when the agreement entered into force.

insights.claim: US FDI stock in Mexico is about $160 billion versus Mexico's ~$60 billion in the US, but normalized for economy size Mexico actually invests more in the US, with more Mexican firms willing to invest if the deal is extended 16 years.
insights.speaker: Geronimo Gutierrez Fernandez, former Mexican Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: A 16-year extension is framed as the unlock for additional Mexican inbound investment into the US; certainty is the trigger for cross-border capital flows.
insights.quote: The U.S. invests around 160 billion dollars... Mexico... has about 60. If you normalize that by the size of the economy, Mexico is actually investing more in the United States

insights.claim: Krugman argues the USMCA has no genuine trade conflict and is a textbook case for going beyond free trade to an EU-style customs union with no border checks and a common external frontier against China.
insights.speaker: Paul Krugman, economist
insights.so_what: Frames the optimal end-state as deeper integration plus a hard China perimeter; a directional read for where pro-trade economists think value lies.
insights.quote: I would say this is a case where we should go beyond free trade to a European Union style customs union with free movement of goods across borders, no checks at all, a common external frontier for goods.

insights.claim: Mexico must 'up its game' on security and geopolitical alignment with the US, signaling that future US market access will be conditioned on alignment against China.
insights.speaker: Geronimo Gutierrez Fernandez, former Mexican Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: Trade access becoming contingent on security alignment raises the bar for all US trading partners; a structural shift from pure-economics trade deals.
insights.quote: every country that wants to have access and do business with the United States in the future is going to have to be a little bit more politically and geopolitically aligned with the United States.

insights.claim: If the USMCA lapses, none of the three countries remain bound by its rules on intellectual property, agriculture, services and coordinated customs procedures—not just tariffs—making collapse far more consequential than tariff changes alone.
insights.speaker: Kirsten Hillman, former Canadian Ambassador to the US
insights.so_what: Reframes deal risk beyond tariffs to the entire rules architecture of $2T in trade; a true lapse would be a systemic, not marginal, shock.
insights.quote: the USMCA contains all the rules on intellectual property protection, all the rules on agriculture, all the rules on services trade... If it goes away, none of us are bound by those rules anymore.
